---
title: "1.2 Variables and Data Types"
subtitle: "Using Objects and Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## What Is a Variable?

### A named place that can change

A <span class="term">variable</span> (变量) is a memory location that stores a value that can change while the program runs.

![Pong score example](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-2-variables-and-data-types/pongScore.png){fig-align="center" width="44%"}

Scores, ages, prices, and names are useful because the same program can work with <span class="mark">different stored values</span>.

## Data Types

### What kind of value can this variable hold?

Java variables need both a <span class="term">name</span> and a <span class="term">data type</span>.

::: {.tight-list}
- `int`: whole numbers
- `double`: decimal values
- `boolean`: `true` or `false`
- `String`: text inside double quotes
:::

AP CSA distinguishes between <span class="term">primitive</span> values and <span class="term">reference</span> types.

## Type Choice

### Match the data to the question

Use these classroom heuristics:

::: {.tight-list}
- average grade -> <span class="mark">`double`</span>
- number of people -> <span class="mark">`int`</span>
- first name -> <span class="mark">`String`</span>
- raining or not -> <span class="mark">`boolean`</span>
:::

Students usually struggle most when they choose `int` for a quantity that really needs decimals.

## Declaring Variables

### Type first, then name

```java
int score;
score = 4;

double price = 23.25;
boolean won = false;
String name = "Jose";
```

To <span class="term">declare</span> a variable is to create it. To <span class="term">initialize</span> it is to give it its first value.

## Memory and Type Size

### Java needs to know how much space to reserve

![Variable types and memory](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-2-variables-and-data-types/typesAndSpace.png){fig-align="center" width="78%"}

::: {.tight-list}
- `int` uses <span class="mark">32 bits</span>
- `double` uses <span class="mark">64 bits</span>
- type affects both storage and allowed operations
:::

## Printing Variables

### Print the value, not the variable name

```java
int score = 0;
String name = "Jose";

System.out.println(score);
System.out.println("Hi " + name);
```

Never put a variable inside quotes if you want its value. `"name"` prints the letters `n a m e`; `name` prints the stored text.

## Naming Variables

### Meaningful, valid, and readable

::: {.tight-list}
- start with a letter
- use letters, digits, and `_`
- no spaces
- do not use Java keywords such as `class`, `if`, `int`
- prefer <span class="term">camelCase</span> such as `gameScore`
- Java is <span class="mark">case-sensitive</span>: `gameScore` and `gamescore` are different
:::

Good names explain the data. `score` is useful; `x` is usually too vague.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- quick checks on choosing the right data type
- a case-sensitivity debugging task
- <span class="term">1.2.5 Debugging Challenge: Weather Report</span>
- <span class="term">1.2.6 Coding Challenge: Mad Libs</span>
:::

These tasks reinforce both <span class="mark">declaration</span> and <span class="mark">meaningful naming</span>.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- define a <span class="term">variable</span> as a named storage location
- identify `int`, `double`, `boolean`, and `String`
- declare and initialize a variable correctly
- print variables without putting them inside quotes
- use clear <span class="term">camelCase</span> names and respect case sensitivity
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
